paragraphs.body: Title: From Pittsburgh to Shanghai: A Tale of Two Airports Main point: Airports are not just gateways to cities but also reflections of culture. By comparing the architectural styles and route networks of PVG and PIT, this narrative examines how design philosophies and traveler experiences reflect the cultural values of China and the United States.

Background: I never heard “Pittsburgh” until I started study at Pitt. The first time I heard this city. I imaged it was a small industrial town with closed factories and shabby buildings. Afterwards when I learned that I would need to transfer from JFK to PIT, my imagination became even more uncertain. “匹兹堡” (the Chinese name of Pittsburgh) made me envision it as a modest, rural-style facilities which small and unassuming in scale.

Based on my stereotypical impression of early American airports, I automatically assumed this would be a small regional airport with a compact terminal, where it would take no more than 30 minutes from arrive to baggage claim. Therefore, I scheduled my Uber just 30 minutes after landing.

Besides, I believed that since Pittsburgh is located in the inland eastern region of the United States, far from major metropolitan areas like New York or Los Angeles, it might not be as 'cosmopolitan.' Therefore, I didn't expect to see Chinese signage at the regional airport - I assumed it would primarily cater to domestic travelers. Conflict: I was shocked when I arrived at Pittsburgh International Airport. I expected a small regional airport, but the airport turned out to be much bigger, with 4 runways and 2 terminals. I was surprised to see a British Airways airplane, and I later learned that this airport provides regular direct flights to the UK. After landing, I even took an underground train to another terminal, showing that the terminal was much larger than I had imagined. The airport signs were in English and Spanish, which I expected, but I was really intrigued to see they were also in Chinese. Seeing things like "行李提取" written in Chinese piqued my curiosity. Rising action: The experience at Pittsburgh International Airport (PIT) reminded me of Shanghai Pudong International Airport (PVG). Both airports are international hubs with multilingual signs in English, Chinese, and Spanish, and they both offer direct flights to the UK. However, PVG's terminals are much larger and more modern, with a wider range of international routes, while PIT mainly serves domestic flights with fewer international options. The types of stores in the two airports are also quite different. Pudong has luxury brands like Louis Vuitton, while Pittsburgh focuses more on convenience stores and restaurants like Dunkin'. Additionally, Pudong's terminal includes traditional Chinese design elements, while Pittsburgh highlights its local culture with displays of the Pittsburgh Steelers and other regional emblems. Climax: Since lack of familiarity with this region, I have to judge the airport's scale based on the city's recognition. However, my experience completely overturned this assumption. PIT was larger and more modern than I imagined, and it even having direct flights to the UK. Compared to Pudong International Airport, PIT stands out not through its scale but through its unique local character, such as displays featuring the Steelers, while PVG places greater emphasis on global and international design. This experience taught me that airports are not merely transportation hubs but also reflections of regional culture and values. It also reminded me of the importance of not making preconceived judgments about anything. Resolution: This comparison made me realize that having preconceived ideas about new things can block true understanding. Observing Pittsburgh International Airport (PIT) and Shanghai Pudong International Airport (PVG) showed me how cultural differences shape airport design and function. Each place expresses its values in its own way, and all deserve respect and appreciation.
